title: Podman run from systemd service as non-root user cannot login to private registry
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I have service `foo` which is using Podman and this service runs as systemd service as user `foo`. When I login as user `foo`, I can safely login into my company's private container registry. However, when I trigger a service to perform login, it fails with `Permission denied` error. **I have confirmed that credentials are working fine.** I also did `loginctl enable-linger foo` - did not help.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run some service as non-root user `foo`

2. Run `loginctl enable-linger foo`

3. Trigger service to perform `podman login`

**Describe the results you received:**

`Permission denied` erorr when logging into registry

**Describe the results you expected:**

Login to private registry successfully 

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

I ran `podman login` with `--log-level debug` and here' the output
```
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="Reading configuration file \"/home/foo/.config/containers/libpod.conf\""
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="Merged system config \"/home/foo/.config/containers/libpod.conf\": &{{false false false true true true} 0 {   [] [] []} /home/foo/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes docker://  runc map[runc:[/usr/bin/runc /usr/sbin/runc /usr/local/bin/runc /usr/local/sbin/runc /sbin/runc /bin/runc /usr/lib/cri-o-runc/sbin/runc]] [] [] [] [/usr/libexec/podman/conmon /usr/local/lib/podman/conmon /usr/bin/conmon /usr/sbin/conmon /usr/local/bin/conmon /usr/local/sbin/conmon] [PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin] cgroupfs /usr/libexec/podman/catatonit /home/foo/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod /tmp/run-500/libpod/tmp -1 false /etc/cni/net.d/ [/usr/libexec/cni /usr/lib/cni /usr/local/lib/cni /opt/cni/bin]  []   k8s.gcr.io/pause:3.1 /pause true true  2048  journald  ctrl-p,ctrl-q false}"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=warning msg="The cgroups manager is set to systemd but there is no systemd user session available"

 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="Failed to add podman to systemd sandbox cgroup: dial unix /run/user/0/bus: connect: permission denied"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=info msg="running as rootless"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="Returning credentials from /run/user/500/containers/auth.json"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="error accessing certs directory due to permissions: stat /etc/docker/certs.d/my.private.registry:5000: permission denied"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="Looking for TLS certificates and private keys in /etc/docker/certs.d/my.private.registry:5000"

 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="GET https://my.private.registry:5000/v2/"

 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="GET https://my.private.registry:5000/v1/_ping"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="Ping https://my.private.registry:5000/v1/_ping err Get https://my.private.registry:5000/v1/_ping: Forbidden (&url.Error{Op:\"Get\", URL:\"https://my.private.registry:5000/v1/_ping\", Err:(*errors.errorString)(0xc00028e210)})"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=error msg="error authenticating creds for \"my.private.registry:5000\": error pinging docker registry my.private.registry:5000: Get https://my.private.registry:5000/v2/: Forbidden"
 Removed login credentials for my.private.registry:5000
```

I do not know if this is important but UID and GID of user foo differ. UID: 500, GID: 498.
Also, docker is installed on this machine as well.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:            1.6.4
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.12.12
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
podman-1.6.4-16.el7_8.x86_64
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

CentOS 7, virtual machine

**Some additional info**

I'm pretty sure that there's something very simple that I do not see yet. Maybe some additional configuration of the service or Podman. It feels like service is not running login session `su -l` and thus fails but I do not know how check that.

comments.body:
Thanks for opening the issue!

>  time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="error accessing certs directory due to permissions: stat /etc/docker/certs.d/my.private.registry:5000: permission denied"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="Looking for TLS certificates and private keys in /etc/docker/certs.d/my.private.registry:5000"
 time="2020-05-26T08:52:46+02:00" level=debug msg="Skipping scan of /etc/docker/certs.d/my.private.registry:5000 due to permission error: open /etc/docker/certs.d/my.private.registry:5000: permission denied"

It looks like the non-root user can't access the certificates. If you change permissions and allow `foo` to access the directory, does it work?

Thanks for rapid response, @vrothberg 

It's strange that Podman tries to look into this directory since it does not exist. I have `/etc/docker/` but no `certs.d` in there.

My company *do* require certificate to access registry but it is placed under `/etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/` and is accessible by user `foo`. I also run `update-ca-trust` when I installed certificate.

You're absolutely right, and after another coffee it's not at all a certs issue. "Forbidden" implies that the registry was reached but access was denied.

How did you confirm that the credentials work?

I did log in into registry using *exact* credentials used by service. The only difference I can think of is that I logged in using `su -l foo`, which create login session and service may not used that.
That might be the missing part. @giuseppe, @rhatdan, aren't there other issues w.r.t. using `su`?

one other issue with `su` is that it doesn't create a proper user session, so we fallback to using `/tmp` instead or `/run/user`.  `/tmp` is periodically cleaned up by systemd-tmpfiles, so it is really usable for short sessions.  Also, if there is no dbus session we cannot create cgroups for rootless containers.

Hi @giuseppe 
It may be a silly question but is there any documentation regarding using Podman in systemd service? I guess the problem is, as you said, with no D-Bus session.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@vrothberg Are we ok with Documentation now?

> @vrothberg Are we ok with Documentation now?

Not yet. I want to wait for the --sd-notify PR to merge. Once that's done, we can create a living doc upstream.

Also, after the PR we can debloat the units from generate systemd quite a lot.
I would like to resolve issue. The reason was misconfigured proxy settings.
